codeSnapshot.label: gat_node_classification.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch_geometric.nn import GATConv
from torch_geometric.datasets import Planetoid

dataset = Planetoid(root='/tmp/Cora', name='Cora')
data    = dataset[0]

class GAT(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super().__init__()
        # Layer 1: 8 heads, each of dimension 8 => output 64
        self.conv1 = GATConv(in_channels, 8, heads=8, dropout=0.6)
        # Layer 2: 1 head (multi-head average) for classification
        self.conv2 = GATConv(8 * 8, out_channels, heads=1, concat=False, dropout=0.6)

    def forward(self, x, edge_index):
        x = F.dropout(x, p=0.6, training=self.training)
        x = F.elu(self.conv1(x, edge_index))        # [N, 64]
        x = F.dropout(x, p=0.6, training=self.training)
        return self.conv2(x, edge_index)            # [N, num_classes]

device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
model  = GAT(dataset.num_features, dataset.num_classes).to(device)
data   = data.to(device)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.005, weight_decay=5e-4)

for epoch in range(200):
    model.train(); optimizer.zero_grad()
    out  = model(data.x, data.edge_index)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(out[data.train_mask], data.y[data.train_mask])
    loss.backward(); optimizer.step()

model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    pred = model(data.x, data.edge_index).argmax(dim=1)
    acc  = (pred[data.test_mask] == data.y[data.test_mask]).float().mean()
print(f"Test accuracy: {acc.item():.4f}")  # ~0.83

eli3: GCN treats all neighbours equally — it just averages their features. But not all neighbours are equally important. Your best friend's recommendation is more valuable than a stranger's. GAT (Graph Attention Network) learns to assign different importance weights to different neighbours, depending on the features of both the node and its neighbour.

The attention mechanism: for each pair of connected nodes, compute a score measuring how compatible their features are. Turn these scores into probabilities using softmax (so they sum to 1 across neighbours). Then take a weighted sum of neighbour features using these probabilities. The model learns what makes a neighbour more or less important for the task.

This attention is content-based: two nodes with similar features will attend to each other strongly, while dissimilar nodes attend weakly. Unlike GCN where the aggregation weights are fixed by graph degree, GAT's weights are dynamic and input-dependent — different inputs produce different attention patterns.

whenWhy.title: Node classification with heterogeneous neighbourhoods
whenWhy.body: When a node's neighbours vary in relevance (e.g., in a citation network, a paper has citations from closely related work and from tangentially related work), fixed-weight aggregation (GCN, GraphSAGE mean) blurs these distinctions. GAT's learned attention assigns higher weights to the most relevant neighbours. This is especially valuable in heterogeneous graphs where different edge types carry different information and must be weighted differently.

whenWhy.title: Inductive learning with attention interpretability
whenWhy.body: GAT's attention weights α_{ij} can be inspected to understand which neighbours influenced a node's representation. In molecular graphs, high-attention edges often correspond to chemically important bonds; in citation networks, they correspond to the most relevant citations. This interpretability is valuable in regulated domains (drug discovery, finance) where model reasoning must be explainable.

whenWhy.title: Knowledge graph completion and link prediction
whenWhy.body: Relational GAT models (R-GAT) assign separate attention heads to different edge types in knowledge graphs (Person → worksAt → Company). This allows the model to weight relationships by type: a 'worksAt' edge contributes differently to the representation of a company node than a 'locatedIn' edge. GAT-based link prediction models achieve state-of-the-art on KBC benchmarks (FB15k-237, WN18RR) by combining relation-specific transformations with attention-weighted aggregation.

deepDiveIntro: Veličković et al. (2018) introduced Graph Attention Networks with learnable, edge-wise attention coefficients. For each edge, the model computes an un-normalized attention score by applying a LeakyReLU nonlinearity to a learned linear combination of the concatenated source and destination node features. Softmax over a node's neighborhood turns these raw scores into weights that sum to one. The updated representation of each node is a weighted sum of its neighbors' projected features, with optional multi-head aggregation. Because attention weights depend only on node features and not on graph topology, GAT generalizes inductively to unseen nodes. [1]

GATv2 (Brody et al., 2021) identified a fundamental limitation in the original GAT: the attention score factors into a sum of a term that depends only on the source node and a term that depends only on the destination. This means the ranking of a node's neighbors by attention weight is the same regardless of the query node's own features — so-called static attention. GATv2 fixes this by applying the nonlinearity before the final dot product, creating a non-separable score that lets the attention ranking vary with both endpoints. On tasks requiring flexible attention, GATv2 consistently outperforms GAT with the same parameter count. [2]
formula (GAT): α_ij = softmax_j( LeakyReLU( a^T [Wh_i || Wh_j] ) )

deepDive.heading: Attention Score Computation
deepDive.body: For each directed edge, the model projects both endpoint features into a shared space, concatenates them, passes the result through a LeakyReLU, and takes a dot product with a learned vector to get a raw attention score. Applying softmax over all neighbors normalizes these scores into probabilities. The updated node embedding is then a probability-weighted sum of neighbor features. This approach lets the model learn which neighbors matter most for each node, rather than treating all neighbors equally as graph convolution does. [1]
deepDive.formula: α_ij = softmax_j( LeakyReLU( a^T [Wh_i || Wh_j] ) ),    h'_i = σ( Σ_j α_ij W h_j )

deepDive.heading: Multi-Head Attention in GAT
deepDive.body: Running K independent attention heads in parallel and concatenating their outputs gives the model K different views of each node's neighborhood. Hidden layers use concatenation so the dimensionality grows by a factor of K; the final layer averages the heads instead to produce a fixed-size output. More heads generally improve accuracy on heterogeneous graphs up to a point, after which compute cost grows without further benefit. [1]
deepDive.formula: h'_i = ||_{k=1}^K σ( Σ_j α^k_ij W^k h_j ),    final: h'_i = σ( 1/K Σ_k Σ_j α^k_ij W^k h_j )

deepDive.heading: GAT vs GCN: When Does Attention Help?
deepDive.body: GAT outperforms GCN when: (1) node neighbourhoods are heterogeneous — different neighbours have different levels of relevance; (2) the graph is noisy — edges may connect dissimilar nodes (noise edges in social networks, false citations); attention down-weights noisy neighbours. GAT is comparable to or slightly below GCN when: (1) all neighbours are equally relevant — attention converges to uniform, adding overhead without benefit; (2) the graph is regular (all nodes have the same degree) — no benefit from degree-normalisation differences. Empirical results: GAT outperforms GCN on Cora (83% vs 81.5%) and Citeseer (72.5% vs 70.3%), but the difference is small and task-dependent. For large-scale graphs with heterogeneous edge types, attention provides more substantial improvements. [2]

deepDive.heading: Sparse Attention and Computational Cost
deepDive.body: For sparse graphs, attention over neighbors is much cheaper than all-to-all Transformer attention because the number of edges is far smaller than the square of the number of nodes. For dense graphs such as social networks with very high-degree nodes, GAT becomes expensive. Graph transformers add structural encodings such as shortest-path distances to standard attention, combining the expressiveness of attention with the efficiency of graph sparsity while maintaining linear-in-edges complexity for sparse graphs.

takeaways: GAT: α_{ij} = softmax(LeakyReLU(a^T[Wh_i||Wh_j])); attention weight replaces GCN's fixed degree normalisation. [1]
Multi-head GAT: K independent attention heads, concatenated in hidden layers and averaged in the final layer. [1]
GATv2 fixes static attention via e_{ij} = a^T LeakyReLU(W[h_i||h_j]) — dynamic, non-separable scoring. [2]
GAT outperforms GCN when neighbours are heterogeneous; comparable when all neighbours are equally relevant. [2]
Attention adds K×|E| overhead over GCN; efficient for sparse graphs but expensive for high-degree nodes. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the static attention problem in GAT and how does GATv2 fix it?
edgeCases.a: In original GAT, e_{ij} = a^T[Wh_i || Wh_j] = a_left^T Wh_i + a_right^T Wh_j. This is a sum of a term depending only on i and a term depending only on j — fully separable. Consequence: for any fixed query node i, the ranking of neighbours by attention score is always the same regardless of what h_i is — it's determined solely by a_right^T Wh_j. The attention is 'static' in the sense that the same neighbours always receive the same relative attention for any choice of i. GATv2 introduces a non-separable score: e_{ij} = a^T LeakyReLU(W[h_i || h_j]). The non-linearity (LeakyReLU applied before the dot product with a) prevents separability — the ranking of j's neighbours now depends jointly on both h_i and h_j. This makes GAT expressive enough to implement any ranking function over neighbours.

edgeCases.q: How does GAT handle nodes with very large or very small degree?
edgeCases.a: For high-degree nodes (hubs): GAT computes softmax over all K neighbours — each neighbour gets weight 1/K on average, reducing to near-uniform attention for very large neighbourhoods (the same limitation as mean aggregation). This can be problematic if only a few neighbours are truly relevant. Mitigation: graph sampling — sample a fixed maximum number of neighbours before computing attention. For low-degree nodes (1–2 neighbours): softmax over 1–2 candidates concentrates all attention on the single most similar neighbour, potentially over-fitting to one edge. GCN's degree normalisation handles this gracefully (1/√d_i is larger for low-degree nodes, emphasising self-features). For graphs with extreme degree distributions (power-law), pre-processing with degree normalisation before GAT is often beneficial.

sources.label: Veličković et al. (2018) — Graph Attention Networks, ICLR 2018 arXiv:1710.10903
Brody, Alon & Yahav (2021) — How Attentive are Graph Attention Networks? (GATv2) ICLR 2022 arXiv:2105.14491
